mnemonics.title: SGD STEP
mnemonics.content: SGD STEP: Stochastic Gradient Descent updates parameters using Single Training Example, learning rate (Step size) controls update magnitude, True gradient estimated from sample, Expected value equals gradient, Probability of finding good solution increases with epochs.

mnemonics.title: MOMENTUM HELPS CONVERGE
mnemonics.content: MOMENTUM: Momentum Overcomes Misguided Oscillations Through Updated Gradients; New direction combines current gradient with fraction of previous direction.

mnemonics.title: LEARNING RATE SCHEDULES
mnemonics.content: SCHEDULES: Step Decay, Cosine Annealing, Heavy-ball, Exponential Decay, Polynomial, Linear, Warm restarts, On Plateau, Square root, End.

acronyms.usage: Optimization algorithm that uses single sample to compute gradient at each iteration
SGD variant combining momentum with per-parameter adaptive learning rates
SGD variant that adapts learning rate based on historical gradient magnitudes
SGD variant using moving average of squared gradients for adaptive learning rates
Momentum variant that computes gradient at lookahead position for better updates

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a ball rolling down a hill. SGD gives the ball a push in the direction of steepest descent at each tiny step. The learning rate controls how big each push is. Too big = ball overshoots valley; Too small = ball moves too slowly.

Think of a heavy ball rolling down a hill. Once it gains speed (momentum), it keeps moving in the same direction and can roll through small bumps. The momentum term accumulates past gradients like velocity accumulates past pushes.

Picture a photographer zooming out gradually. Initially, large steps help move quickly toward the minimum. Later, smaller steps fine-tune the position to avoid overshooting. Decay shrinks step size over time.

Imagine trying to find the lowest point in a foggy mountain by feeling the ground under your feet at one spot at a time. Each step gives you a noisy estimate of the true downhill direction. On average, you still go downhill but the path is zigzaggy.

analogies.analogy: Comparing a person searching for the lowest point in a dark terrain: Batch GD is like studying the entire terrain map before taking one step (slow but accurate direction). SGD is like taking one step in a random direction, feeling the slope, then stepping again (fast but zigzaggy path).

Like a driver adjusting speed: High learning rate = highway driving (fast but dangerous, might miss exits). Low learning rate = crawling (safe but takes forever). Decay = starting fast then slowing down as you approach destination.

Like a bicycle coasting downhill: Once you gain speed (momentum), you don't need to pedal hard at every tiny bump. Past momentum carries you forward, and you only need to steer (adjust direction with current gradient).

Like reading a few pages (mini-batch) of a book rather than the whole book at once (batch GD) or one word at a time (pure SGD). Balance between understanding context (accurate gradient) and finishing the book (computational efficiency).
